front: Definition of Ready (DoR) vs Definition of Done (DoD) — what does each gate, and who primarily owns each?
back: DoR: criteria for a backlog item to enter a sprint/iteration (team + product owner collaboration). DoD: criteria for a product increment to be considered complete/potentially releasable (team-owned quality bar). DoR protects planning; DoD protects quality and releasability.
tip: Entry gate = Ready; exit/quality gate = Done. DoD is a Scrum commitment; DoR is common practice, not Scrum-mandated.

front: Scrum Team accountabilities (2020): who are the three, and what is each primarily accountable for?
back: Product Owner — maximize product value and own Product Backlog content/order. Developers — create a usable Increment each Sprint. Scrum Master — establish Scrum, coach the team/org, and remove impediments (servant-leader).
tip: 2020 Guide: accountabilities, not hierarchical “roles”; no separate “Development Team.”

front: Who may cancel a Sprint, and on what basis?
back: Only the Product Owner may cancel a Sprint—if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete (e.g., major market/tech shift). Completed Done items can still be released; unfinished work returns to the Product Backlog.
tip: Exam trap: SM or sponsor does not cancel the Sprint—PO owns that call.

front: Map each Scrum artifact to its commitment.
back: Product Backlog → Product Goal. Sprint Backlog → Sprint Goal. Increment → Definition of Done.
tip: Three artifacts, three commitments—high-yield 2020 Scrum mapping.

front: What belongs in the Sprint Backlog, and who owns it?
back: The Sprint Goal, the selected Product Backlog items for the Sprint, and an actionable plan for delivering the Increment. Owned and updated by the Developers (emergent plan).
tip: PO owns Product Backlog; Developers own Sprint Backlog.

front: What makes work a valid Increment in Scrum?
back: A concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal that is usable and meets the Definition of Done. Multiple Increments may be created in a Sprint; the Increment is inspectable at or before Sprint Review.
tip: If it isn’t Done, it is not part of the Increment—no partial credit on the exam.

front: List the five Scrum events and the max timeboxes for a one-month Sprint.
back: Sprint (container, ≤1 month). Sprint Planning (≤8 h). Daily Scrum (≤15 min). Sprint Review (≤4 h). Sprint Retrospective (≤3 h). Shorter Sprints use proportionally shorter events.
tip: All events are formal opportunities to inspect and adapt; timeboxes protect focus.

front: Sprint Planning: what three topics/questions structure the event?
back: Why is this Sprint valuable? (Sprint Goal—PO proposes value; team crafts Goal). What can be Done this Sprint? (select backlog items). How will the chosen work get done? (Developers plan the work).
tip: Why / What / How—full Scrum Team collaborates; Developers own the “how.”

front: Daily Scrum: who must attend, what is the purpose, and what is it NOT?
back: Developers required (others may attend if useful). Purpose: inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog for the next 24 hours. Not a status report to the SM/PM or a problem-solving workshop.
tip: If the answer turns it into PM status theater, reject it.

front: Sprint Review vs Sprint Retrospective — primary focus and typical attendees (beyond the basic distinction).
back: Review: inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog with stakeholders (Scrum Team + key stakeholders); outcome is a revised Product Backlog. Retro: Scrum Team only inspects people/process/tools and agrees on improvements for the next Sprint.
tip: Review = product/value with stakeholders; Retro = process with the team only.

front: Product Owner vs project sponsor/customer: how should the PMP exam treat backlog priority conflicts?
back: The PO is the single voice of priority for the Product Backlog. Sponsors/customers influence value and constraints, but competing requests are ordered through the PO—not by the team taking orders from multiple bosses mid-Sprint.
tip: One Product Owner, one ordered backlog—hybrid projects still need a clear product decision owner.

front: Scrum Master as servant-leader: what do they do for the team, PO, and organization?
back: Team: coach self-management, remove impediments, protect Scrum. PO: help with backlog techniques, clear goals, stakeholder collaboration. Org: lead Scrum adoption, coach managers/stakeholders, remove systemic barriers.
tip: SM enables outcomes; does not assign tasks or own delivery like a command-and-control PM.

front: Servant-leader cues vs command-and-control cues on agile/hybrid PMP items—what does the exam favor?
back: Favor: facilitate, coach, remove impediments, ask the team, protect focus, empower decisions. Avoid as first move: dictate tasks, unilaterally re-prioritize Sprint work, skip team input, or “take over” technical solutions when the team can decide.
tip: 2026 ECO People domain: lead by enabling the team, not by hierarchical control.

front: Velocity vs capacity — definitions and how each is used in planning.
back: Velocity: historical throughput of Done work per Sprint (often story points)—a forecast input, not a target/commitment weapon. Capacity: available effort this iteration after leave, meetings, support (usually hours/person-days). Plan with capacity for who is available; use velocity to forecast how much similar work fits.
tip: Weaponizing velocity (demand more points) is an anti-pattern; capacity explains this Sprint’s reality.

front: Backlog refinement (grooming): purpose, who participates, and roughly how much effort?
back: Ongoing activity (not a formal Scrum event) to add detail, estimates, and order so upcoming items meet readiness for planning. PO + Developers (SM coaches as needed). Common rule of thumb: up to ~10% of team capacity—enough that Sprint Planning is not discovery chaos.
tip: Refinement feeds DoR; it does not replace Sprint Planning’s commitment to a Sprint Goal.

front: Kanban vs Scrum on roles, cadence, and flow control—what must you know for PMP?
back: Scrum: fixed roles/accountabilities, timeboxed Sprints and events, pull work into a Sprint commitment. Kanban: no required roles/sprints; visualize workflow, limit WIP, manage flow (cycle time/throughput), continuous delivery/pull. Hybrid often uses Scrum events with Kanban WIP limits on the board.
tip: Kanban core: visualize, limit WIP, manage flow, make policies explicit, improve collaboratively.
